svg.text: How the CLI, daemon (master / agent), web client + wasm, MCP, backend-client, broker, workers, signer and chain fit together. Companion to the §2 inventory table.
Wire shapes have ONE owner — agentkeys-protocol — which both the native backend-client and the browser web-core (wasm) depend on, so a body can't drift across native vs browser.
daemon → cli · Rust lib (agent_admin pending pass-through)
cap-mint + worker calls → via backend-client (both planes)
identity / session (J1) ← broker at init (CLI / core)
AGENT SANDBOX — remote runtime · VM / CI / cloud-LLM
BROKER HOST — remote cloud · origin/main deploy
K1 cap co-sign · K2 OIDC JWT (→ AWS STS)
verifies scope / registry / epoch on chain
never holds K3 · no AWS principal at runtime
creds · memory · config · classify · audit · email
each INDEPENDENTLY re-verifies the cap on chain
test broker (mock-server) · devops TUI (fleet) — off the request path
K3 vault (sealed) · KEK derive · STS mint
network call (HTTPS / localhost) — the request path
mTLS to the signer TEE (typed RPC, signer-protocol)
shared wire types — agentkeys-protocol is the one owner (no native vs wasm drift)
in-process dependency (Rust crate link / JS module import) — compiles in, no network
Both daemon planes (master + agent) reach the broker / workers
from the broker at init (CLI / core).
Headline: every cap is re-verified on chain by the worker before
any S3 / KEK / STS op. Boundaries & keys: arch §3–§4.
